events.event: The Battle of the Somme begins; the bloodiest day in the history of the British Army.
The investiture of Prince Charles as Prince of Wales at Caernarfon Castle.
The United Kingdom transfers sovereignty of Hong Kong to the People's Republic of China.
The Representation of the People Act 1928 receives Royal Assent, giving women equal voting rights to men.
The Battle of Marston Moor: The Roundheads and Scots defeat the Royalists in the English Civil War.
The birth of Stephen Mangan, the English actor and comedian.
The world speed record for a steam locomotive is set by 'Mallard' at 126 mph in Lincolnshire.
The first issue of the satirical magazine 'Punch' is published in London.
The British Royal Navy attacks the French fleet at Mers-el-Kébir to prevent it falling into German hands.
Lewis Carroll first tells the story of Alice's Adventures in Wonderland during a boat trip on the Thames.
The National Health Service (NHS) is established by Health Secretary Aneurin Bevan.
Marie Curie dies; her research on radioactivity profoundly influenced British medical science.
Isaac Newton's 'Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica' is published, revolutionising physics.
The first post-war General Election is held in the UK, resulting in a Labour landslide.
The National Health Service officially comes into operation.
Sir Thomas More is executed for treason after refusing to acknowledge Henry VIII as head of the Church of England.
The Piper Alpha oil platform in the North Sea is destroyed by explosions, killing 167 people.
Louis Pasteur successfully tests his rabies vaccine; the method was quickly adopted in the UK.
Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, the creator of Sherlock Holmes, dies in Crowborough.
Coordinated suicide bomb attacks on London's public transport system kill 52 people.
The birth of Ringo Starr, the legendary drummer of The Beatles.
The death of Percy Bysshe Shelley, the major English Romantic poet, in Italy.
The birth of Ellen MacArthur, the English sailor who broke the world record for solo circumnavigation.
The first issue of The Wall Street Journal is published; its financial reporting influenced the City of London.
The Russell-Einstein Manifesto is issued in London, highlighting the dangers of nuclear weapons.
The first Wimbledon Championships begins at the All England Croquet and Lawn Tennis Club.
The engagement of Princess Elizabeth and Lieutenant Philip Mountbatten is announced.
The Battle of Britain officially begins as the Luftwaffe attacks British convoys in the Channel.
Lady Jane Grey is proclaimed Queen of England, reigning for only nine days.
The first parking meters in Britain are installed in Mayfair, London.
The Irish War of Independence ends with a truce between the British and Irish forces.
The 1966 FIFA World Cup begins in England.
The birth of John Higgins, the Scottish professional snooker player.
Henry VIII marries his sixth and final wife, Catherine Parr, at Hampton Court Palace.
The Battle of the Boyne takes place in Ireland (Old Style date).
The Rolling Stones play their first ever gig at the Marquee Club in London.
Live Aid concerts are held at Wembley Stadium and JFK Stadium to raise money for famine relief.
The Hollywood sign is officially dedicated; its influence on British cinema is immense.
The birth of Ian Hislop, the English journalist and editor of Private Eye.
The birth of Emmeline Pankhurst, the leader of the British suffragette movement.
The death of Billy the Kid; tales of the American West became a staple of British popular culture.
The birth of Nick Clegg, the former Deputy Prime Minister of the UK.
The First Crusade: Christian soldiers capture Jerusalem, involving many English knights.
The Duke of Monmouth is executed for leading a rebellion against James II.
The birth of Ray Fearon, the acclaimed British stage and screen actor.
The Trinity nuclear test occurs; British scientists participated via the Manhattan Project.
The Romanov family is executed in Russia; King George V was the Tsar's cousin.
The birth of Joshua Reynolds, the influential English painter (New Style date).
King George V issues a proclamation changing the name of the British Royal House from Saxe-Coburg and Gotha to Windsor.
The Potsdam Conference begins, with Winston Churchill representing the UK.
The birth of Robin Cousins, the English figure skater and Olympic champion.
The death of Jane Austen, the celebrated English novelist, in Winchester.
The Ballot Act 1872 introduces the secret ballot for elections in the UK.
The birth of James Brolin; his roles became familiar to UK television audiences.
The Mary Rose, Henry VIII's flagship, sinks in the Solent during an engagement with the French fleet.
The Spanish Armada is sighted off the coast of Cornwall.
The birth of Alan Price, the English musician and member of The Animals.
The Apollo 11 moon landing is broadcast live on the BBC, a major cultural moment in the UK.
The Hyde Park and Regent's Park bombings are carried out by the IRA, killing 11 soldiers.
The July Plot to assassinate Hitler; the event was closely monitored by British intelligence.
The death of Robert Burns, the national poet of Scotland.
The birth of John Atta Mills, the Ghanaian politician who studied at LSE and SOAS in London.
The birth of Louis Armand, who was instrumental in the development of the Eurotunnel project.
The Battle of Falkirk: Edward I defeats the Scots under William Wallace.
The birth of Prince George of Cambridge at St Mary's Hospital, London.
The birth of Bob Dole; the American statesman worked closely with British diplomats.
Boris Johnson is elected leader of the Conservative Party, becoming Prime Minister the following day.
The birth of David Gedge, the English musician and leader of The Wedding Present.
Austria-Hungary issues an ultimatum to Serbia, leading to the UK's entry into WWI.
Mary, Queen of Scots, is forced to abdicate in favour of her infant son, James VI.
Hiram Bingham discovers Machu Picchu; the feat inspired many British archaeologists.
The birth of Jonathan Hill, the British politician and European Commissioner.
Louise Brown, the world's first 'test-tube baby', is born in Oldham, Greater Manchester.
Louis Blériot becomes the first person to fly across the English Channel.
The birth of Matt LeBlanc; the actor's work on 'Friends' and 'Top Gear' made him a UK household name.
Clement Attlee's Labour Party wins the general election in a landslide victory.
The Suez Crisis begins when Egyptian President Nasser nationalises the Suez Canal.
The birth of George Bernard Shaw, the influential Irish playwright and polemicist active in London.
The opening ceremony of the London 2012 Olympic Games takes place.
The Sexual Offences Act 1967 decriminilises homosexual acts in private in England and Wales.
The Bank of England is established by Royal Charter.
Thomas Cromwell is executed on the same day Henry VIII marries Catherine Howard.
World War I officially begins with Austria-Hungary's declaration of war on Serbia.
The death of Jim Corbett, the British hunter and conservationist famous for his books on India.
The wedding of Prince Charles and Lady Diana Spencer takes place at St Paul's Cathedral.
The Battle of Gravelines: The Spanish Armada is defeated by the English fleet.
The birth of Richard Hammond, the English journalist and broadcaster.
England win the FIFA World Cup, defeating West Germany 4-2 at Wembley.
The birth of Emily Brontë, author of 'Wuthering Heights', in Yorkshire.
The death of William Penn, the English Quaker who founded Pennsylvania.
Black Tot Day: The last daily rum ration is issued to sailors in the Royal Navy.
The birth of J.K. Rowling, the creator of the Harry Potter series.
Daniel Defoe is placed in a pillory for seditious libel; the crowd pelts him with flowers.
